claim: Private credit may be a net systemic risk reducer rather than amplifier by shifting risk from leveraged, runnable banks to locked-up long-duration capital
needs: Empirical stress test at $1.7T scale; quantification of insurance/pension loss absorption capacity; analysis of BDC/interval fund redemption channels and thresholds; assessment of second-order political and regulatory channels from insurance/pension losses

claim: The deficit-term premium reflexive loop is activating and inherently destabilizing, with net interest rising from ~$350B to ~$1T
needs: US-specific activation threshold calibration given reserve currency status; empirical estimation of the recycling coefficient (10-25% range too wide); comparison with UK gilt crisis dynamics accounting for structural differences; monitoring of the blended borrowing rate vs nominal GDP growth margin

claim: SEC central clearing mandate (Q2 2026) is a calendar-identifiable catalyst for basis trade position adjustment
needs: Corroboration from other agents; assessment of regulatory implementation probability and timeline; analysis of whether market is already front-running; quantification of expected position adjustment magnitude

claim: US fiscal expansion simultaneously supports DM credit quality and tightens EM conditions through the same channel (Kalecki EM paradox)
needs: Decomposition of EM tightening into US fiscal channel vs other dollar drivers; cross-agent corroboration; quantification of the relative magnitude of DM support vs EM tightening from fiscal channel alone

claim: Japan's experience (fiscal support sustained economy but not asset prices — equity -60%+ despite deficits) is a cautionary parallel for the US Kalecki thesis
needs: Structural comparison of US vs Japan corporate leverage distribution; assessment of whether private credit losses could produce Japan-like multi-year loss recognition; analysis of why the Kalecki channel sustained Japan's economy but not asset prices; cross-agent engagement with this counter-evidence

claim: Reserve adequacy frameworks (Guidotti-Greenspan, IMF ARA) may be structurally broken if Treasuries become illiquid during stress
needs: Quantification of actual EM central bank reserve diversification away from Treasuries; stress testing of reserve adequacy under Treasury dysfunction scenarios; empirical analysis beyond the single March 2020 episode; assessment of alternative reserve assets' liquidity during simultaneous stress
